content: Laser cleaning of steel has emerged as a game-changing technology across industries like automotive, aerospace, shipbuilding, construction, and heavy machinery manufacturing, offering precision and efficiency over traditional methods like abrasive blasting or chemical treatments. This process utilizes high-intensity laser beams, typically pulsed fiber lasers with power outputs ranging from 100W to 2000W, to ablate contaminants such as rust, paint, or oxide layers from steel surfaces without damaging the substrate. In automotive manufacturing, laser cleaning preps steel panels for welding or coating, ensuring superior adhesion—think spot cleaning on assembly lines for high-strength steel components. Aerospace applications demand flawless surface integrity; here, lasers remove corrosion from critical steel parts like landing gear struts without altering metallurgical properties. Shipbuilding benefits from cleaning massive steel hulls, tackling marine-grade corrosion, while construction and heavy machinery sectors use it for refurbishing structural beams or excavator frames. Though not flawless—laser systems can struggle with deep crevices or uneven surfaces—the technology minimizes material loss and environmental impact, unlike sandblasting’s waste. For optimal results, parameters like pulse duration (often 10-100 ns) and scanning speed must be tailored to steel grade and contaminant type, with testing on sample surfaces highly advised to prevent over-heating or under-cleaning.
